coredata.dc:description: Chronic diabetic cutaneous wounds resulting from inflammatory conditions present an ongoing challenge for current therapies and impose a significant burden on individuals with diabetes, impacting their quality of life. Infection-related diabetic skin wounds require dry conditions to inhibit bacterial growth. However, as the wounds progress, moisture becomes necessary to facilitate the healing process. In this study, we propose a novel therapeutic strategy for diabetic skin repair by creating bio-dressings with adjustable “hydrophobic” and “hydrophilic” characteristics to accommodate the changing stages of the disease. We developed a skin dressing by loading calcium peroxide (CaO2) nanoparticles onto carbon dots (CD)-modified irradiated zein (Ir-Zein). This dressing releases reactive oxygen species (ROS) from CaO2, providing antibacterial effects, while the presence of CD enables a sustained release of CaO2. The calcium ions produced by CaO2 degradation further promote skin regeneration. Ir-Zein protein, a cost-effective and easily processed natural plant protein, exhibits excellent biocompatibility. Importantly, in diabetic rats with full-thickness skin defects, the CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein film significantly accelerated the healing of chronic wounds. Mechanistic investigations revealed that the film effectively reduced inflammation by inhibiting the polarization of macrophages towards the M1 phenotype and capturing pro-inflammatory cytokines. In summary, our findings demonstrate the effectiveness of the CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein film’s “adaptive hydrophobicity-to-hydrophilicity” in promoting the transition of chronic wounds from the inflammatory stage and skin repair. CaO2/CD@Ir Zein is a novel bio-dressing that can adapt to the changing environment of infected diabetic skin wound healing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic overview of the preparation procedures of the CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein film dressing and the mechanism of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein in promoting chronic diabetic wound healing.

Characterization of different materials. (A) SEM images of nano CaO2. (B) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CaO2. (C–E) SEM images of Ir-Zein, CD@Ir-Zein and CaO2/Ir-Zein (CaO2 nanoparticles are highlighted with red circles in Fig. 2E). (F–H) Diameter distribution of the three bioflims. (I) C, O, N, Ca elements distribution map of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
(A–I) The water contact angles of materials.

Antibacterial properties of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein. (A, B) MIC and MBC values of Ir-Zein, CD@Ir-Zein, and CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein against S. aureus, E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and B. subtilis. (C) Results of bacteriostatic rates. (D, E) Quantification of the average diameters of inhibition zones. (F) Results of biofilm destruction.

CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein inhibits bacterial growth via ROS production. (A–D) ROS determination of different materials. (E–H) Results of alamar blue cell viability test. (I) Results of agar plate validation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Biocompatibility of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein in vitro. (A) The CCK-8 assay of Raw264.7 macrophages incubated with different material leaching solution for 24 h. (B) The DAPI/Phalloidin staining of Raw264.7 macrophages cultured in different material leaching solution for 24 h. The nuclei were stained with DAPI in blue fluorescence, and the cytoskeleton was stained with rhodamine phalloidin in red fluorescence. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

In vivo assessment of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein for diabetic wound healing in rat. (A) The representative images of full-thickness skin wounds after treatment with control, Ir-Zein, CD@Ir-Zein, and CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein. (B) Change of wound closure percentages with time. (C) H&E staining of the wound on day 10. (D) Quantitative analysis of scar widths in re-epithelialization. (E) Masson’s trichrome staining of tissue regeneration within the wound on day 10. (F) Quantitative analysis of mean intensity.

Polarization of macrophages and anti-inflammatory effects of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein. (A) Immunofluorescence staining of rat skin tissue for CD68 (pan-macrophage marker) and CD86 (M1 macrophage marker). (B) Quantification of CD68 and CD86 expression from immunofluorescence staining. (C–E) The expression level of inflammatory cytokines TNF-α, IL-1β, and IL-6 in Raw264.7 macrophages.

head.ce:title: Electron irradiation of zein protein-loaded nano CaO2/CD for enhancing infectious diabetic wounds with adaptive hydrophobicity-to-hydrophilicity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chronic diabetic cutaneous wounds resulting from inflammatory conditions present an ongoing challenge for current therapies and impose a significant burden on individuals with diabetes, impacting their quality of life. Infection-related diabetic skin wounds require dry conditions to inhibit bacterial growth. However, as the wounds progress, moisture becomes necessary to facilitate the healing process. In this study, we propose a novel therapeutic strategy for diabetic skin repair by creating bio-dressings with adjustable “hydrophobic” and “hydrophilic” characteristics to accommodate the changing stages of the disease. We developed a skin dressing by loading calcium peroxide (CaO2) nanoparticles onto carbon dots (CD)-modified irradiated zein (Ir-Zein). This dressing releases reactive oxygen species (ROS) from CaO2, providing antibacterial effects, while the presence of CD enables a sustained release of CaO2. The calcium ions produced by CaO2 degradation further promote skin regeneration. Ir-Zein protein, a cost-effective and easily processed natural plant protein, exhibits excellent biocompatibility. Importantly, in diabetic rats with full-thickness skin defects, the CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein film significantly accelerated the healing of chronic wounds. Mechanistic investigations revealed that the film effectively reduced inflammation by inhibiting the polarization of macrophages towards the M1 phenotype and capturing pro-inflammatory cytokines. In summary, our findings demonstrate the effectiveness of the CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein film’s “adaptive hydrophobicity-to-hydrophilicity” in promoting the transition of chronic wounds from the inflammatory stage and skin repair. CaO2/CD@Ir Zein is a novel bio-dressing that can adapt to the changing environment of infected diabetic skin wound healing.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The incidence of diabetes is rapidly increasing [1,2], with some estimates predicting more than 590 million afflicted people by 2035. Clinically, the high-glucose microenvironment plays an important role in the occurrence and evolution of diabetic wound [3–5]. Chronic cutaneous wounds induced by inflammatory conditions are usually difficult to heal and constitute a substantial burden for people with diabetes and impact on quality of life [6,7].

As one of the most vital immune cells in combating infection during the early inflammatory phase, macrophages can become overactivated and impaired in their function due to prolonged stress caused by systemic inflammation triggered by hyperglycemia [8,9]. Consequently, it could lead to an increased production and release of inflammatory factors, intensifying the inflammatory response and resulting in a loss of control [10]. This impedes the normal progression from the inflammatory phase to subsequent proliferative and maturation phases, ultimately resulting in the development of chronic, non-healing wounds [11,12]. Furthermore, the hyperglycemic environment, characterized by reduced host immunity and long-term inflammation, provides favorable conditions for the proliferation of microorganisms [13]. Bacteria are more likely to survive and reproduce in high blood sugar environments, leading to the formation of biofilms in skin wounds [14–16].

Disrupting and removing biofilm is an effective strategy to inhibit bacteria from contaminated and infected wounds, thereby promoting wound healing [17–19]. Physical irrigation or surgery is the preferred clinical treatment for biofilm-associated infections, but these methods can cause significant impairment and pain to patients. However, conservative therapy using conventional wound dressings are usually ineffective in disrupting mature biofilm [20,21]. Therefore, a novel antibacterial wound dressing with high efficacy in biofilm disruption is necessary [22,23]. Recently, nanoantimicrobial technology has experienced rapid development in the antibacterial field [24–26]. Additionally, diabetic wound as a chronic disease could be characterized by a long course of disease, inevitably, impose the economic burdens on patients and their families [27]. Zein, a natural plant protein, has been widely utilized to develop biocompatible films in biomedical and tissue engineering applications, which could be more easily accessible in underdeveloped regions [28]. Due to its high porosity, excellent biocompatibility and biodegradability, cost-effectiveness and easy processability, zein has been considered to be a favorable candidate for wound dressing and skin tissue engineering [29,30].

Reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation and antimicrobial therapy have been currently proposed to accelerate the recovery of diabetic wound [31,32]. Oxidative stress induced by ROS in bacteria can lead to bacteria wall and membrane breakdown, and subsequently leading to bacterial death [33–35]. Calcium peroxide (CaO2), a metal peroxide with high biocompatibility and effective oxygen release ability, has great potential for antibacterial applications in medical implants as the release of oxygen and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) provided by hydrolysis of CaO2 could be accelerated within the acidic microenvironment of bacterial infection. Besides, the functions and roles of calcium in skin regeneration and reconstruction during diabetic would healing have been identified [36]. Calcium could expedite the healing process by modulating wound angiogenesis [37], promoting fibroblast migration [38], and enhancing collagen synthesis [39]. Nevertheless, due to the aqueous instability of CaO2, the applicability of its antibacterial effect is limited [40]. To solve this problem, we synthesized CaO2 nanoparticles (CaO2 NPs) and loaded them onto the combination of conventional zein films and carbon dots (CD) with electron beam irradiation for sustained release. CD is also reported to exhibit antibacterial properties with complex mechanisms [41]. Antibacterial activities of CD predominantly involve disruption of cell wall and dispersal of biofilm matrix with ROS generation [42].

Moreover, the repair of typical imperfections requires maintaining a moist environment [43–45], while the humidity requirements for the wound environment can vary during different stages of repairing diabetes-related defects [46–48]. For instance, Pseudomonas aeruginosa would be prone to thriving and forming biofilms in humid environments, which hinder wound repair [49]. Additionally, Staphylococcus aureus could proliferate quickly in humid conditions, generating toxins that induce tissue damage and disrupt the natural wound healing process [50]. With this in mind, we have developed a wound dressing with a unique “adaptive hydrophobicity-to-hydrophilicity” feature. This characteristic makes the initial dressing more hydrophobic, effectively balancing desiccation and discouraging bacterial growth. As CaO2 and CD degrade following the initial antibacterial phase, the dressing becomes more hydrophilic, leading to improved preservation of wound moisture and facilitating wound healing.

In this study, a novel antibacterial wound dressing CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein is developed for the first time, in which CD-modified irradiated zein is used as a carrier to load CaO2 nanoparticles (Fig. 1 ). The aim of this study is to examine the biodegradability, ROS level, antibacterial properties, and potent anti-inflammatory activity of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein to confirm its excellent promotion to wound healing.

Calcium chloride (CaCl2,99%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2,30%), ammonia solution (NH4OH, 28-30%), polyethylene glycol 200 (PEG 200), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and glacial acetic acid (CH3COOH, 99%) were purchased from Sinopharm. Ethanol (C2H5OH, ≥99.7%) was obtained from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. Zearalytic protein was obtained from Dalian Meilun Biotech Co., Ltd. Ingredients from Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), Escherichia coli (E. coli), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis) and Luria–Bertani (LB) medium were purchased from Guangzhou Huankai Biotech Co., Ltd. Alamar Blue Assay Kit, ROS Assay, L-929 Fibroblast, Raw264.7 macrophage, Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM), Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) and Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) were provided by Shanghai Fanmeng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. All reagents were used directly, and deionized water was used throughout the experiment.

Commercially available Assam tea was heated in a muffle oven at a set 100 °C for 2 h. Afterwards, it was ground to a powder form and underwent further heating at 200 °C for approximately 8 h. The resulting black carbide powder was then cooled and stored in a glass vial at room temperature. 300 mg of carbide tea powder was then dispersed in a medium of 10 mL of 0.1 M acetic acid and allowed to remain undisturbed for 40 h. The dispersed medium was subsequently centrifuged at a rate of 10,000 rpm for 0.5 h. Finally, the supernatant containing tea carbon quantum dots was collected and preserved.

The CaCl2 (3 g) was dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water, and the mixture was continuously stirred for 30 min. Afterwards, 15 mL of ammonia solution (1 M) and 120 mL of PEG 200 were added. While stirring the mixture for 2 h, 15 mL of 30% H2O2 was slowly added at a rate of 3 drops per minute. The solution gradually changed color from colorless to yellowish during this process. The pH of the system was adjusted to 11.5 by adding NaOH solution of pH = 13, resulting in a white color pellet. The pellet was then collected by centrifugation and rinsed three times with NaOH solution and deionized water, respectively. Finally, it was dried in a vacuum drying chamber at 80 °C for 12 h.

Zein prolamine (0.75 g) was dissolved in a mixed solution of 3 mL tea carbon spot and ethanol (volume of tea carbon point: volume of ethanol = 1:3). After irradiating this solution with an electron beam of 80 kGy, a precursor solution was obtained. Next, 300 mg CaO2 was dispersed into the above solution under magnetic stirring until a uniform spinning solution was obtained. Electrospinning parameters were established as follows: a 0.60 mm nozzle, applied voltage of 22 kV, flow rate of 0.02 mL/min, and distance of 15 cm from the tip to the collector. The prepared nanofibers by electrospinning were denoted by CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein in contrast to the materials prepared without the addition of tea carbon dots, which were represented as CD@Ir-Zein. In addition, the fiber membranes prepared without the addition of CaO2 and tea carbon dots were pure irradiated Zein.

The morphology of CaO2 nanoparticles and nanofilms was observed using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss Sigma 300). The nanofilms were characterized at room temperature on a FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS5) with a scanning wavenumber ranging from 4000 to 500 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of the prepared samples was performed using a Rigaku Ultima IV instrument, while X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CaO2 was analyzed using a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI instrument. To assess the thermal stability of samples, the thermal behavior of samples with different blend ratios of nanofilms was examined by Thermo Gravimetric Analysis (TGA) using a PerkinElmer 8000 instrument with samples being heated from 25 to 600 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The mechanical properties of all samples were measured in compliance with ASTM D256 standard using a universal testing machine (CMT6103, MTS, USA) with a speed of 1 mm/min. Sample dimensions were 40 × 10 mm2 and the thickness was kept the same. The sample clamping distance was set at 20 mm, and each sample was tested three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free radical (H2O2) and oxygen releasing experiments
ce:section.ce:para: The release of H2O2 was assessed using an H2O2 assay kit (ab272537, Abcam, USA). Initially, 4 × 4 cm2 square samples were immersed in 60 mL of 0.01 M buffer solution. Following specific intervals (30 s, 1 min, 2 min, 5 min, 10 min, 20 min, 30 min, 60 min, 90 min), 2 mL of liquid was aspirated with a syringe, then centrifuged at 600 r/min in a centrifuge tube for 2 min. Subsequently, clear liquid was extracted and added into 96-well plates, incubating at room temperature for 30 min. Post-incubation, the samples were scrutinized using a microplate reader at an absorbance of 585 nm. H2O2 releasing was quantified utilizing a standard curve equation.

The release of oxygen was assessed by measuring the dissolved oxygen content at various time points. Ir-Zein (300 mg), CD@Ir-Zein (300 mg), and CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein (300 mg) were placed in brown 50 mL centrifuge tubes, and 30 mL of PBS (0.01 M, pH = 7.4) was added to each tube. The tubes were then shaken at 37 °C at 120 rpm, and 3 mL of supernatant was tested at specific time intervals (2 h, 6 h, 12 h, 24 h, 72 h, 120 h, 168 h, and 216 h). Following this, 3 mL of fresh PBS was added again. The dissolved oxygen content in the extract was measured utilizing a dissolved oxygen meter (oxi330i).

2 μL of deionized water droplets were dropped onto the nanofibers, and the contact angle of each droplet was measured using an automated optical contact measuring instrument (Chengde Dingsheng Testing Machine Testing Equipment Co. Ltd).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) and minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC)
ce:section.ce:para: The MIC and MBC of the prepared material were determined through broth dilution. Ir-Zein, CD@Ir-Zein, and CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein were dissolved in a co-solvent (volume of ethanol: volume of water = 1:1) with a concentration of 320 mg/mL and then diluted sequentially with a nutritious broth to concentrations of 160, 80, 40, 20, 10, and 5 mg/L. The sample solution (500 μL) was supplemented with 100 μL of bacterial suspension (1 × 109 CFU mL−1) and 4.4 mL of nutritious broth. For the negative control, the sample solution was replaced with a 500 μL mixed water/ethanol solution. For the blank control, 4.9 mL of nutritious broth and 100 μL of bacterial suspension were used. The bacterial fluid was then incubated in a 37 °C shaking incubator for 24 h, and the MIC was defined as the lowest concentration with no visible bacterial growth (no significant change in turbidity at 595 nm compared to blank controls). To determine the MBC, 100 μL of the bacterial solution was coated on an agar plate and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The lowest concentration with bacterial-free growth on agar plates was recorded as the MBC.

The bacteriostatic rate testing was conducted using our previously reported method [51]. The 4 × 4 cm2 square samples were immersed in a 60 mL microbial suspension at 37 °C and incubated for 18 h. Following that, the bacteria were collected by centrifugation at a rate of 1000 r/min in a 5 mL solution in a centrifuge tube. The collected bacteria were weighed after cooling and drying. Lastly, the bacteriostatic rate was determined by calculating the difference in the test tubes' weight.

The antibacterial mechanism of the material was investigated in vitro by examining the morphology of bacterial cells (S. aureus) through SEM. All processed samples were fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde, left overnight, and rinsed thrice with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS). Subsequently, they underwent dehydration using a gradient of ethanol concentrations (30%, 50%, 70%, 80%, 90%, 95%, and 100%). The samples were then treated with a 1:1 mixture of ethanol and isoamyl acetate for 30 min, followed by exposure to pure isoamyl acetate for 1 h. After critical point drying, they were gold-coated through sputtering. Finally, SEM was employed to observe the bacterial morphology.

The antibacterial activity of complex fibers was assessed through agar diffusion experiments, utilizing S. aureus, E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and B. subtilis as test organisms. The bacterial suspension (1 × 107 CFU mL−1), consisting of 60 μL, was applied to the Luria-Bertani (LB) agar plate. Following that, sample discs (sterilized with ultraviolet light for 15 min prior to the experiment) were evenly distributed on the plates and incubated at 37 °C for 18 h. Lastly, the bacteriostatic ring was measured and documented via photography.

The 18 × 18 mm2 coverslips were washed with phosphate buffer and dried naturally for sterilization. Four coverslips were taken and placed on solid medium, inoculated with 2 mL of bacterial suspension (OD = 0.2) and bacterial suspension containing MIC concentration material, respectively. Then, the coverslips were placed in a 37 °C biochemical incubator for 48 h. The biofilm cultured on the coverslip was washed three times with PBS to remove planktonic bacteria. Next, crystal violet drops were added to the coverslip for staining for 15 min. After that, the coverslip was rinsed twice with PBS to wash off the excess crystal violet, and finally washed off the colored crystal violet with 33% acetic acid. The resulting solution was transferred to a six-well plate and tested for absorbance using a microplate reader at 595 nm.

In a 12-well plate, various treatments including Ir-Zein, N-Acetylcysteine (NAC) at a concentration of 10 mM, CD@Ir-Zein, MIC doses of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein, and CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein +10 mM NAC were applied to bacterial solutions containing 5 × 105 CFU per well, followed by incubation at 37 °C for 3 h. Then, DCFH-DA (10 μM) was added to each well and incubated at 37 °C for 20 min prior to analysis using a fluorescence microplate reader.

In a 96-well plate, treatments including Ir-Zein, 10 mM NAC, CD@Ir-Zein, CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein, and CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein +10 mM NAC were added to the bacterial suspension (100 mL, 5 × 104 CFU mL−1). Then, add 10 μL of alamar blue reagent to each well and incubate in the dark at 37 °C for 4 h. Afterwards, measure the fluorescence intensity using a microplate reader with 545 nm and 590 nm wavelength emission. Evaluate cell viability by using a standard curve (%).

The CCK-8 assay was used to assess the cytotoxic performance of the prepared films. The nanofilm samples, which were sterilized with ultraviolet light before the experiment and washed with PBS, were immersed in each well of a 24-well polystyrene plate, together with the intact medium. At the same time, Raw264.7 macrophages and fibroblasts with a density of 5 × 103 cells/well were respectively seeded into 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator. The cell culture medium was then replaced with the leaching fluids from the different membranes described above. The method used to obtain the material leaching solution was based on our previously published literature [52]. The culture was then incubated for another 24 h. Afterwards, 10 μL of CCK-8 reagent was added to a 96-well plate and incubated. The assay was performed in a microplate reader at 450 nm. Three sets of experiments were carried out per sample, with all results analyzed for average viability ± standard deviation (SD) of n = 3.

Raw264.7 macrophages were cultured onto cell sheets in 24-well plates with a cell density of 1.0 × 105 cells per well for 24 h. Afterwards, the leaching fluids from various membranes specified above were added to the cell culture medium. To evaluate the adherence and proliferation of Raw264.7 macrophages, 4′,6-diamidino-2phenylindole (DAPI) staining was used to mark nuclei (blue) and rhodamine phalloidin was used to show the cytoskeleton of Raw264.7 macrophages (red) at 24 h. All images were captured using an Olympus IX81 microscope with a UC90 camera.

For this study, we selected male Sprague-Dawley (SD) rats that were six weeks old and weighed approximately 200 g. The rats were randomly divided into four groups: the blank control group, the carrier control group (Ir-Zein), the carbon dot group (CD@Ir-Zein), and the drug loading group (CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein). Each group was housed in one cage with three rats per cage. The animals were purchased and raised in an SPF level facility with a constant indoor temperature of 23 ± 2 °C, constant humidity of 50 ± 5%, and a 12-h day and night cycle. They had free access to sterile feed and drinking water. We used a full-thickness skin defect model of diabetic rat to implant corresponding materials in the skin defects according to the above groupings to study their mechanism of action on antibacterial and wound healing.

Streptozotocin (STZ) was relatively less toxic to the body’s tissues, and the survival rate of animals was high. Therefore, it was currently used at home and abroad to prepare an animal model of diabetes. The specific experimental steps for making the animal model were as follows:
1.
Take 6-week-old SD rats and weigh them after overnight fasting. Prepare a 1% STZ (Solarbio, China) citric acid buffer (65 mg/kg, pH = 4.2–4.5), filter it through a 0.22 μm filter, and inject it into the corresponding rats based on their fasting body weight. Administer the injection as a rapid bolus and complete it within 30 min.
2.
Monitor the behavioral performance of rats after injection. If rats experience fasting-induced hypoglycemia or develop ketoacidosis, provide an appropriate amount of insulin therapy.
3.
To prevent infection, replace the bedding of diabetic rats once a day, as they tend to produce a large amount of urine that can make the bedding wet.
4.
Monitor blood glucose levels through the tail vein. A venous blood glucose value of 13.9–16.7 mmol/L was generally considered a successful model. If the value was not up to standard, supplement the STZ after 3 days (intraperitoneal injection at a body weight dose of 10–20 mg/kg).

After inducing diabetes in rats, intraperitoneal injection of 1% pentobarbital at a dose of 0.1 mL/100 g body weight was administered for anesthesia. After anesthesia, the rats were fixed in a prone position on the board and the hair on the back and around the rats was shaved with an electric shaver. The back skin of each rat was then disinfected and a circular full-layer skin defect with a 15 mm diameter was created. After 12 h of ultraviolet irradiation disinfection, the dressings of each experimental and control group were placed on the defect, and sterile gauze was covered for 1 day before removal. The healing status of the defect model was observed for 10 consecutive days, with skin healing observed during the operation and in 3, 6, and 10 days after surgery (using Huawei P40). The percentage of healing skin in the original defect area was quantified using Image J software. After 10 days of postoperative overdose anesthesia, the materials were taken after the rats' death, and follow-up H&E staining, Masson and Gram staining were performed to evaluate the repair effect of the antibacterial dressing on the epidermis of diabetic rats.

ce:section.ce:section-title: H&E staining, masson staining and gram staining
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained entire layer of skin tissue was fixed in a 4% formaldehyde (PBS dilution) solution for 48 h and then rinsed overnight with running water. Following gradient dehydration, xylene transparency, paraffin embedding and slicing, the resulting sections (with a thickness of 5 μm) were dewaxed with water. Tissue morphology and structural changes were then observed using routine H&E, Masson and Gram staining. The staining reagent used was purchased from Servicebio (China).

Rat skin tissue sections were stained with primary CD68 (pan-macrophage marker), CD86 (M1 macrophage marker), and CD206 (M2 macrophage marker) antibodies at 4 °C overnight, then incubated with secondary antibodies for 10 min followed by three rounds of DAPI staining, each lasting 5 min, at room temperature. The stained slides were scanned using the TissueFAXS platform (TissueGnostics, Vienna. Austria) at 20 × magnification. The quantification of stained samples, which involved measuring expression levels, utilized the StrataQuest software (version 7.1.129, TissueGnostics GmbH, Vienna, Austria).

Following co-culture, Raw264.7 macrophages were trypsinized, rinsed, and incubated with Brilliant Violet 605 anti-mouse CD206 (BioLegend, San Diego, CA) at 4 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, cells were washed once more and examined using flow cytometry (BD FACS Aria™ II cell sorter; BD Biosciences) to evaluate the expression of their surface epitopes.

Following the recovery of Raw264.7 macrophage line, incubation and culture took place by adding DMEM, which was supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% double antibody (Penicillin-streptomycin, Invitrogen, USA). In a 6-well plate, 1 × 106 Raw264.7 cells were introduced, and after complete adherence to the wall, the solution was changed to Ir-Zein, CD@Ir-Zein, CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein lixivium and/or 100 ng/mL lipopolysaccharide (LPS, Sigma Aldrich, USA), respectively. After 24 h, EZ press RNA purification kit (EZbioscience, China) was used to extract whole RNA, which was quantitatively measured for mRNA expression after reverse transcription on a QuantStudio 7 instrument (Thermofisher, USA). All primers were provided by BioTNT (China) and the sequences were as follows: GAPDH, forward: 5-TGTTTCCTCGTCCGTGTAGA-3, reverse: 5-ATCTCCACTTGCCACTGC-3; TNF-α, forward: 5-CGCTGAGGTCAATCTGC-3, reverse: 5-GGCTGGGTAGAGAATGGA-3; IL-1β, forward: 5-AGTTGACGGACCCCAAA-3, reverse: 5-TCTTGTTGATGTGCTGCTG-3; IL-6, forward: 5-ACAGAAGGAGTGGCTAAGGA-3, reverse: 5-AGGCATAACGCACTAGGTTT-3; CD206, forward: 5-CTCTGTTCAGCTATTGGACGC-3, reverse: 5-CGGAATTTCTGGGATTCAGCTTC-3; IL-10, forward: 5-AGGCGCTGTCATCGATTTCT-3, reverse: 5-TGGAGTCCAGCAGACTCAAT-3.

All the data was quantified using GraphPad Prism software. For two independent samples, we analyzed variance homogeneity with an F-test followed by a corrective t-test. For multi-group independent sample-to-sample difference analysis, we used one-way ANOVA to determine if the difference was solely between groups. We then used Tukey’s post hoc test or Dunnett’s T3 post hoc test for pairwise difference analysis based on variance homogeneity. All p-value analyses were bidirectional, and statistical significance was set at ***P < 0.001, **P < 0.01, and *P < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and design strategy of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein
ce:section.ce:para: Recent advances in nanotechnology have spurred the development of metal nanomaterials, particularly gaining increasing attention in cancer treatment strategies [53,54]. In ion-interference therapy (IIT) for tumors, metal ions have been utilized to disrupt intracellular biological processes [55–57]. Zhang et al. [55] found that the in-situ-degraded products of CaO2 NPs include metal ions (Ca2+), anions (OH-), small molecules (H2O2), and clusters, offering unique biological effects and interactions with the tumor microenvironment. Additionally, there have been reports on the potential antimicrobial properties of nanomaterials such as nanoparticles, nanosheets, and nanoplatelets [54,58], involving mechanisms such as ROS production, oxidative stress, and charge transfer [53]. Due to the similarity between bacterial infections and the tumor microenvironment in terms of lower pH levels [59], we speculate that nanoscale CaO2 particles may effectively eliminate bacterial infections in the microenvironment of infectious diabetic skin defects by accelerating the release of H2O2. Furthermore, studies have confirmed that calcium can expedite the healing of skin wounds by modulating wound angiogenesis [37], promoting fibroblast migration [38], and enhancing collagen synthesis [39,60,61]. Therefore, we hypothesize that nanoscale CaO2 particles may play a significant role in the regeneration and reconstruction of infectious diabetic skin defects. CD has demonstrated excellent drug release properties in previous research [62], and CD-modified calcium peroxide particles can achieve slow and stable hydrolysis. Zein is a natural plant protein widely used in the development of biocompatible films for biomedical and tissue engineering applications [28]. It possesses high porosity, outstanding biocompatibility, biodegradability, cost-effectiveness, and ease of processing, making it a favorable candidate for dressings and skin tissue engineering [29,30]. Based on the aforementioned studies, we have successfully prepared nanoscale CaO2 particles and loaded them onto electron beam-irradiated zein modified with CD, with the hope that they can be applied in the treatment of infectious diabetic skin defects.

The SEM image of Fig. 2 A showed that the CaO2 nanoparticles were spherical with a diameter of approximately 15–25 nm. Fig. 2B and Fig. S1A showed the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the CaO2 nanoparticles and the X-ray diffraction (XRD) results of Ir-Zein and CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein, indicating the successful preparation of nanoscale CaO2. The prepared biofilms of Ir-Zein, CD@Ir-Zein and CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein were observed under SEM (Fig. 2C–E), revealing a microstructure with interwoven fibrous patterns in the irradiated zein film, along with the successful attachment of CD modified CaO2. The quantitative analysis of diameter distribution revealed that CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein possessed an aperture structure with an average size of 189 nm, slightly smaller than the other two dressings (Fig. 2F–H). A decrease in particle size might imply improve in vivo biodegradation performance. Fig. 2I demonstrated that the C, O, N, and Ca elements were uniformly dispersed in the nanofibers, revealing the successful embedding of CaO2 nanoparticles. Fig. S1B illustrated that zein demonstrates improved mechanical properties after being irradiated, indicating an extended dressing lifespan and better wound support. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) results in Fig. S1C revealed similar diffraction peaks of Ir-Zein and CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein, implying that the loading of CaO2/CD had not altered the molecular structure of Ir-Zein. The result of thermogravimetric analysis of Zein, Ir-Zein, CD@Ir-Zein, and CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein was displayed in Fig. S1D. Fig. S2 demonstrated the generation of free radical by CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein, while Fig. S3 illustrated variations in the oxygen release rate of CaO2 and dressings with different additives in an in vitro simulated environment.

The repair of typical defects requires maintaining a moist environment, which aids in promoting cell regeneration, reducing scar formation, and providing appropriate protection during wound healing [43–45]. However, it is worth noting that a moist environment is more conducive to bacterial growth [63]. For instance, in certain humid environments, Pseudomonas aeruginosa infections may occur frequently [64]. Research has indicated that changes induced by desiccation in S. aureus biofilms can reduce bacterial load in the wound bed compared to wounds infected with normal biofilms [65]. Therefore, maintaining a relatively dry environment around the wound is an effective adjunct for infection control. Repairing skin defects with concurrent infections necessitates considering both infection control and wound healing aspects. Wound healing in diabetic patients is often compromised by infections. Therefore, adjusting the humidity of the wound environment at different stages of the healing process may be beneficial to meet the varying needs of infection control and wound repair [46–48].

Based on this perspective, we have developed wound dressings with “adaptive hydrophobicity-to-hydrophilicity” characteristic. Electron beam-irradiated zein films exhibited strong hydrophilicity, but after modification with CD, the dressings demonstrated significantly enhanced hydrophobicity. Loading CaO2 further augmented hydrophobicity. Contact angle measurements for dressings at different stages were depicted in Fig. 3A–I. The surface contact angle of Ir-Zein nanofiber film was 101° (Fig. 3 A–C), but within 15 s, all water was absorbed by the nanofibers, reducing the contact angle to 13°, indicating its hydrophilic nature. However, after blending CD into Ir-Zein, the surface contact angle improved, and the shape of the water droplet remained almost unchanged after 15 s, indicating a hydrophobic feature (Fig. 3D–F). The statistical analysis of changes in water contact angles was displayed in Fig. S4. Our research suggested that the enhanced hydrophobicity of the microfibers is primarily attributed to changes in fiber structure in the presence of CaO2 and CD. This characteristic rendered the initial dressing (CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein) more hydrophobic, thus inhibiting bacterial growth and reproduction. Following the degradation of CaO2 and CD, the dressing became more hydrophilic, facilitating the maintenance of wound moisture and promoting wound healing.

Materials designed to adapt to changing humidity based on the requirements of diabetic wound infections may better suit different stages of the diabetic skin defect repair process. The “adaptive hydrophobicity-to-hydrophilicity” feature has the potential to enhance the effectiveness of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein dressings in repairing infectious wounds.

Patients with infectious diabetic skin lesions often struggle with poor blood sugar control. Clinical practice typically involves a combination of oral or intravenous antibiotics, wound debridement, and blood sugar management [66]. Local adjunctive anti-infective therapy remains a more direct and less likely to induce systemic resistance approach to effective treatment. Therefore, dressings with antimicrobial properties have been a focal point of clinical research. Nano CaO2 hydrolysis, releasing ROS, has been reported for the treatment of solid tumors, yielding positive therapeutic outcomes. Research has confirmed that CaO2 possesses some antibacterial activity [67–69]. However, it is yet to be determined whether CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein can effectively inhibit bacteria. In our study, we conducted preliminary in vitro tests to assess the antibacterial effects of Ir-Zein, CD@Ir-Zein, and CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein dressings (Fig. 4 A, B). The results indicated no significant difference between CD@Ir-Zein and Ir-Zein, suggesting that CD@Ir-Zein lacks antibacterial or bactericidal effects. Conversely, CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein exhibited a certain level of antibacterial activity against S. aureus, E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and B. subtilis, with quantitative results demonstrating antibacterial rates of 92.3%, 90.0%, 84.3%, and 83.5%, respectively. These findings underscored its robust antibacterial efficacy, with rates exceeding 80% (Fig. 4C). Experimental results substantiated that CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein exhibited outstanding antibacterial performance. Fig. 4D illustrated the average diameter of the zones of inhibition (DIZ) for Ir-Zein, CD@Ir-Zein, and CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein when tested against four different microbial strains. CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein exhibited potent antibacterial activity against S. aureus, E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and B. subtilis, resulting in inhibition zones measuring 24.7, 36.7, 25.2, and 27.3 mm, respectively (as shown in Fig. 4D and E). These findings underscored the significant impact of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein on the disruption of these four distinct microbial strains. The bactericidal mechanism of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein was further elucidated through alterations in the morphology of S. aureus, as evident in the SEM images (Fig. S5).

Bacteria possess the capacity to form intricate three-dimensional structures known as “biofilms”, which consist of extracellular polymeric matrices [70]. These biofilms display characteristics such as antibiotic resistance, immune evasion, and an affinity for adhering to wounds [71]. Bacteria residing within biofilms can modulate their gene expression to enhance their adaptability to antimicrobial agents and host immune responses, surpassing their planktonic counterparts [72]. In recent decades, there has been a growing recognition of the pivotal role played by biofilm formation in the persistence of bacterial infections [73]. Consequently, conducting bacterial biofilm disruption assays to investigate the potential of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein in disrupting biofilms is of utmost significance. We evaluated the effectiveness of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein dressing in disrupting biofilms through our research studies, which showed potent inhibitory efficacy on the biofilm (Fig. 4F). Conventional dressings, typically employed in conservative treatments, often fall short in effectively dismantling fully developed biofilms [20,21]. In addition to physical flushing or surgical interventions, various strategies such as bacteriophages and enzymes have been proposed and developed for biofilm disruption [74]. However, translating these strategies into clinical practice has been significantly impeded by adverse effects, including, but not limited to, the dissemination of harmful genes, autoimmune responses, and high production costs [75,76]. Based on our findings, CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein shows promise in expediting the clinical translation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein inhibits bacterial growth via ROS production
ce:section.ce:para: Drawing on previous research, it has been firmly established that ROS can trigger a stress response in bacteria, leading to detrimental effects on their cell walls and membranes, ultimately culminating in bacterial demise [33–35]. Given the effective oxygen-releasing capability of CaO2, we postulated that the induction of bacterial death via ROS liberated during its hydrolysis might constitute the primary mechanism driving its antibacterial activity. To substantiate this hypothesis, we conducted an assessment of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein’s capacity to generate ROS within a bacterial solution environment. Furthermore, we introduced the ROS scavenger NAC into the respective culture media to observe its influence on bacterial growth. ROS levels were quantified through DCFH-DA staining. Remarkably, the ROS levels in CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein were substantially elevated compared to conditions lacking CaO2 supplementation (Fig. 5 A–D). Upon NAC treatment, both groups exhibited a noticeable reduction in ROS levels, converging towards those observed in the Ir-Zein and CD@Ir-Zein groups, underscoring NAC’s effective elimination of ROS within the culture medium.

To gain further insights into the ramifications of ROS clearance on colony growth, we conducted alamar blue assays across different groups. The empirical results unequivocally demonstrated a significant decrease in colony viability within the CaO2-doped groups (CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein) (Fig. 5E–H). Post-ROS clearance from the bacterial solution using NAC, colony viability returned to a normal range. In the context of agar plating validation experiments, a conspicuous reduction in colony numbers was observed in the CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein group relative to the Ir-Zein and CD/Ir-Zein groups. However, upon the blockade of ROS generation with NAC, colony growth within the CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein group exhibited a noteworthy acceleration (Fig. 5I). Collectively, these experiments provided compelling evidence that CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein exerts its potent antibacterial activity through the release of ROS.

CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein dressing has been shown to have excellent antibacterial effects, but its potential toxicity to normal cells remains unclear. Macrophages are potent immune effector cells that play significant roles in defending against pathogens and maintaining homeostasis, also contributing importantly to the healing of diabetic skin wounds [77,78]. Besides, fibroblasts contribute to skin wound healing by synthesizing the extracellular matrix and releasing a diverse array of growth factors [79]. To assess the biocompatibility of this dressing, we conducted a CCK-8 assay to evaluate the toxicity of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein on macrophages and fibroblasts. After 24 h of culture, there were no significant differences in cell proliferation levels among the four groups (Fig. 6 A and Fig. S6). Furthermore, we examined the adhesion and proliferation of Raw264.7 macrophages in the leaching solution of the material. To visualize the nuclei and cytoskeletons of Raw264.7 macrophages, DAPI and rhodamine phalloidin staining were performed. After 24 h of culture, Raw264.7 macrophages in the CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein, CD@Ir-Zein, Ir-Zein, and blank groups exhibited well-spread morphology, indicating strong cell vitality (Fig. 6B). These experiments collectively confirm that the Ir-Zein, CD@Ir-Zein, and CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein groups all demonstrate good biocompatibility in vitro.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein enhances diabetic wound healing in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Given the superior antibacterial capabilities and good biocompatibility of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein in vitro, we hypothesized that it would have a therapeutic effect on infectious skin defects. Diabetes patients are at a significantly increased risk of infection [66]. We established a full-thickness skin defect model in diabetic rats to evaluate the role of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein bio-dressing in wound healing potential in susceptible diabetic rats (Fig. 7 A). Fig. S7 illustrated the non-significant differences in body weight changes among rats in various groups over time. Gross photographs on the day of surgery showed that Ir-Zein dressing rapidly absorbed surrounding moisture after implantation, becoming moist, while CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein and CD@Ir-Zein group dressings, due to their better hydrophobicity, could better assist in maintaining the initial wound microenvironment. From the 3rd to the 10th day after surgery, the healing of skin defects in rats treated with CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein were superior to the other three groups. It is worth noting that wounds treated with CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein were almost completely covered with new skin tissue on the 10th day (Fig. 7A). Wound area calculations also indicated that the CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein group outperformed the control, Ir-Zein, and CD@Ir-Zein groups in wound healing (Fig. 7B). To further assess wound healing, we performed H&E, Masson’s trichrome, and Gram staining on full-thickness wound specimens to evaluate epithelialization, collagen deposition, and maturation. It was evident that on the 10th day, keratinocytes almost completely covered the wound in the CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein group, whereas in the control group, there were noticeable gaps in re-epithelialization (Fig. 7C). Quantitative analysis showed that the CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein group had a higher rate of re-epithelialization compared to the control, Ir-Zein and CD@Ir-Zein groups (Fig. 7D). Furthermore, on the 10th day post-surgery, the wounds in the CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein group were fully filled with collagen fibers, arranged and distributed more maturely. In contrast, the collagen qualities in the control and Ir-Zein groups were quite sparse and primitive (Fig. 7E). Masson’s trichrome staining also showed significantly more ordered deposition of collagen and elastic fibers in the CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein group. Quantitative analysis of mean intensity indicated greater mechanical strength in the healing tissue of the CaO2-doped group (Fig. 7F). In addition, the Gram staining tissue image in Fig. S8 reveals the presence of S. aureus in the tissues from the blank, Ir-Zein, and CD@Ir-Zein groups.

The in vivo validation results indicate that CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein exhibits a favorable effect on skin defect repair, closely related to its tunable hydrophilicity-hydrophobicity, excellent biocompatibility, and controlled release of ROS. These findings suggest that it holds great potential as a bio-dressing for infectious skin wounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anti-inflammatory effects of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein via inhibiting M1 macrophage polarization
ce:section.ce:para: Macrophages play a fundamental role in wound healing and the immune process [77,80]. As the primary producers of inflammatory cytokines in wounds, recent research has focused on the function of macrophages in diabetic wound repair [81,82]. Macrophage polarization significantly regulates the inflammatory response during the tissue repair process [83]. During wound healing, tissue repair and remodeling rely on two types of macrophages: classical activation (M1 polarization) and alternative activation (M2 polarization) macrophages [77]. Both of these macrophage types can originate from M0 macrophages. Pro-inflammatory M1 macrophages are known for their production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, such as IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α [84]. The overexpression of these inflammatory factors can lead to delayed healing and adverse tissue remodeling [85,86]. They induce and recruit inflammatory cells by activating the NF-kB pathway [85,86], which keeps the wound in a state of inflammation. The prolonged presence of M1 macrophages has been shown to hinder tissue repair. In diabetic-related impaired healing conditions, wounds exhibit sustained and detrimental inflammatory responses triggered by M1 macrophages, thus impeding the healing process [87].

We assessed the expression of macrophages around diabetic wounds treated with three different dressings. Fluorescent double staining results demonstrated that CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein significantly inhibited the expression of M1 macrophages around diabetic skin wounds (Fig. 8 A, B) compared to the control, Ir-Zein, and CD@Ir-Zein groups. Fig. S9 illustrated the effective polarization of M1 macrophages into M2-like cells treated with CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein dressing, marking the smooth progression of tissue repair and the resolution of inflammation. Flow cytometry analysis also revealed that CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein facilitated the differentiation of macrophages into the M2 phenotype (Figs. S10A and B). Additionally, the expression of typical M2 macrophage markers, CD206 and IL-10, was enhanced in the macrophages of the CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein group (Figs. S10C and D). To further investigate the mechanism by which CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein inhibits M1 macrophage polarization and promotes wound healing, we simulated the inflammatory state of skin ulcers in vitro using LPS. We examined the expression of inflammatory factors in macrophages co-cultured with the three types of dressings. The experiments confirmed that the three dressings did not have a spontaneous pro-inflammatory effect. However, after the addition of LPS, the expression levels of IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α significantly increased in the control, Ir-Zein group, while CD@Ir-Zein and CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein dressings successfully inhibited these inflammatory cytokines, with CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein showing the most significant inhibitory effect (Fig. 8C–E). These results support the hypothesis that CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein is involved in inhibiting the NF-κB pathway and the inflammatory response, thereby promoting the healing process. The outstanding anti-inflammatory properties of CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein may be one of the mechanisms underlying its promotion of wound healing, making it an ideal material for the repair of infectious diabetic skin lesions.

This study introduces a versatile antibacterial wound dressing, CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein, made from a novel CD-modified irradiated zein loaded with CaO2 NPs. Designing a moisture-adjustable skin dressing for susceptible diabetic skin wound healing throughout the entire process is a novel therapeutic strategy. In our research, the wound dressing with unique characteristic of “adaptive hydrophobicity to hydrophilicity” was proven to be potential for antibacterial properties and accelerating the healing of full-thickness skin wounds in diabetic rats. The CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein dressing exerts its antibacterial effects early on by sustained releasing ROS, and its hydrophobic properties further inhibit bacterial proliferation. The degradation-released calcium and zein protein can help promote wound repair. Our research confirms its excellent antibacterial performance and its capability to accelerate wound healing in a full-thickness skin defect diabetic model in vivo. Bio-dressings that adapt their hydrophilic properties to changes in the disease progression of diabetic patients may be more suitable for the treatment of skin defects in infection cases. CaO2/CD@Ir-Zein is a promising biomaterial that can assist in the healing of infected diabetic skin wounds.
